name: Pre-Calculus
description: Bridges Algebra II and Calculus — covering advanced functions, trigonometry, vectors, parametric equations, polar coordinates, conic sections, and an introduction to limits.

modules.title: Functions: Review & Advanced Concepts
modules.objectives: Review domain and range; compose and invert functions; analyze piecewise functions and transformations.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 1.1–1.4: Functions, Domain, Range, and Inverses
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Function Composition
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Functions, Domain, Range, and Inverse Functions
modules.assignment: For 5 pairs of functions find f(g(x)) and g(f(x)). Find the inverse of 4 functions and verify by showing f(f⁻¹(x)) = x. Graph 2 piecewise functions and state their domain and range.

modules.title: Polynomial & Rational Functions
modules.objectives: Graph polynomial functions using end behavior and zeros; identify holes versus vertical asymptotes; construct sign charts for rational functions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 3.3–3.7: Polynomial and Rational Functions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Asymptotes of Rational Functions
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Graphing Polynomial and Rational Functions
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — End Behavior and Rational Functions Practice
modules.assignment: Graph 4 polynomial functions, identifying all zeros and describing end behavior. Analyze 4 rational functions: find all asymptotes, holes, intercepts, and sketch the graph using a sign chart.

modules.title: Exponential & Logarithmic Functions
modules.objectives: Apply properties of exponents and logarithms; solve exponential and logarithmic equations; model logistic growth; use the change-of-base formula.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 4.1–4.7: Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Exponential and Logarithmic Functions (Full Lecture)
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Properties of Logarithms Practice
modules.assignment: Solve 8 exponential equations and 8 logarithmic equations. Use change-of-base to evaluate 4 logarithms on a calculator. Write and analyze a logistic growth model for a real-world population scenario.

modules.title: Right Triangle Trigonometry
modules.objectives: Apply SOH-CAH-TOA to find missing sides and angles; use special angles (30-60-90, 45-45-90); apply inverse trig functions; solve real-world elevation and depression problems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 5.1–5.2: Right Triangle Trigonometry
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Trigonometric Ratios (SOH-CAH-TOA)
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Introduction to Trigonometry and Right Triangles
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Trig Ratios in Right Triangles Practice
modules.assignment: Solve 10 right triangles (find all missing sides and angles). Solve 3 angle-of-elevation/depression application problems. Verify the exact values for all special-angle trig ratios from memory.

modules.title: Unit Circle & Radian Measure
modules.objectives: Convert between degrees and radians; locate reference angles; recall exact trig values from the unit circle; compute arc length and sector area.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 5.2: Unit Circle, Sine, and Cosine
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Unit Circle Definition of Trig Functions
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Unit Circle and Radian Measure (Full Lecture)
modules.assignment: Convert 10 angles from degrees to radians and 10 from radians to degrees. Fill in all 16 standard unit circle points from memory. Compute arc length and sector area for 4 given circles.

modules.title: Graphs of Trig Functions
modules.objectives: Graph sine, cosine, and tangent functions; identify amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift; write equations from graphs.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 6.1–6.2: Graphs of Sine, Cosine, and Tangent
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Amplitude, Midline, and Period of Sinusoidal Functions
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Graphing Trig Functions (Sine, Cosine, Tangent)
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Trig Functions and Their Graphs Practice
modules.assignment: Graph 6 sinusoidal functions (identifying amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift). Write equations to match 4 given sinusoidal graphs. Use Desmos to verify all graphs.

modules.title: Trig Identities & Equations
modules.objectives: Verify Pythagorean, reciprocal, and quotient identities; apply sum/difference and double-angle formulas; solve trigonometric equations on a given interval.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 7.1–7.3: Trig Identities and Equations
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Trigonometric Identities and Equations
modules.assignment: Verify 6 trig identities algebraically. Use sum/difference formulas to find exact values for 4 non-standard angles. Solve 6 trig equations on [0, 2π).

modules.title: Law of Sines & Cosines
modules.objectives: Solve oblique triangles using Law of Sines and Law of Cosines; analyze the ambiguous case (SSA); compute triangle area using Heron's formula and the ½ab sin C formula.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 8.1–8.2: Law of Sines and Law of Cosines
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Law of Sines and Law of Cosines
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Law of Cosines Practice
modules.assignment: Solve 6 triangles using Law of Sines and 4 using Law of Cosines. Analyze 3 ambiguous SSA cases — determine how many triangles exist. Compute the area of each solved triangle.

modules.title: Vectors
modules.objectives: Represent vectors in component form; compute magnitude and direction angle; perform vector addition and scalar multiplication; calculate the dot product and determine orthogonality.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 8.8: Vectors in Two Dimensions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Vectors and Scalars
modules.video2Note: 3Blue1Brown — Vectors, What Even Are They? (Essence of Linear Algebra)
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Vector Magnitude and Direction Practice
modules.assignment: Write 6 vectors in component form from magnitude/direction and vice versa. Perform vector addition, subtraction, and scalar multiplication for 4 sets of vectors. Compute 4 dot products and determine whether each pair of vectors is orthogonal.

modules.title: Parametric Equations & Polar Coordinates
modules.objectives: Graph parametric curves by eliminating the parameter; convert between rectangular and polar coordinates; graph polar equations; recognize common polar curves.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 8.6–8.7: Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Parametric Equations
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Polar and Rectangular Coordinates Practice
modules.assignment: Graph 4 parametric curves by hand and verify using Desmos. Convert 6 points from polar to rectangular and 6 from rectangular to polar. Identify and sketch 3 common polar curves (circle, cardioid, rose).

modules.title: Conic Sections (Advanced)
modules.objectives: Write and graph conic sections in standard and general form; identify focus, directrix, vertices, and eccentricity for parabolas, ellipses, and hyperbolas; classify conics from the general equation.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 10.3–10.5: Parabola, Ellipse, Hyperbola
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Conic Sections
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Conic Sections (Parabola, Ellipse, Hyperbola)
modules.assignment: Write equations for 2 parabolas, 2 ellipses, and 2 hyperbolas given key features. Convert 4 conic equations from general form to standard form by completing the square. State focus and eccentricity for each.

modules.title: Sequences, Series & Mathematical Induction
modules.titleZh: 数列、级数与数学归纳法
modules.objectives: Identify and generate arithmetic and geometric sequences; compute partial sums of arithmetic and geometric series; prove statements using mathematical induction.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 11.1–11.4: Sequences, Series, and Mathematical Induction
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Sequences, Series, and Mathematical Induction
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Arithmetic and Geometric Series Practice
modules.assignment: Find the nth term and the sum of the first 10 terms for 4 arithmetic sequences and 4 geometric sequences. Write and complete 2 proofs by mathematical induction (e.g., sum of first n integers, divisibility).

modules.title: Probability & Combinatorics (Advanced)
modules.objectives: Count arrangements using permutations and combinations; expand binomials using the Binomial Theorem and Pascal's triangle; compute theoretical probability for multi-step experiments.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 11.5–11.7: Counting, Binomial Theorem, Probability
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Combinatorics and the Binomial Theorem
modules.assignment: Solve 6 permutation and 6 combination problems. Expand 4 binomial expressions using the Binomial Theorem. Use Pascal's triangle to find a specific term in a binomial expansion. Solve 3 probability problems involving combinations.

modules.title: Introduction to Limits
modules.objectives: Describe the intuitive definition of a limit; evaluate limits graphically and numerically; compute one-sided limits; apply the basic limit laws; determine continuity at a point.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 12.1–12.4: Limits and Continuity
modules.video2Note: 3Blue1Brown — Limits, L'Hôpital's Rule, and Epsilon Delta Definitions
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Estimating Limits from Graphs Practice
modules.assignment: Evaluate 8 limits graphically. Compute 8 limits algebraically using limit laws (direct substitution, factoring, rationalizing). Identify 3 points of discontinuity on given graphs and classify each as removable, jump, or infinite.

questions.question: If f(x) = 2x + 1 and g(x) = x², what is f(g(3))?
Which of the following is the inverse of f(x) = (x − 3)/2?
questions.explanation: Swap x and y: x = (y − 3)/2 → 2x = y − 3 → y = 2x + 3.

questions.question: A rational function has a hole at x = 2. This means the factor (x − 2) appears in:
questions.explanation: A hole (removable discontinuity) occurs when a common factor cancels from both numerator and denominator.

questions.question: Solve for x: log₂(x) = 5.
questions.explanation: log₂(x) = 5 means 2⁵ = x = 32.

questions.question: In a right triangle, if the angle θ = 30° and the hypotenuse = 10, what is the side opposite to θ?
questions.explanation: sin 30° = opposite/hypotenuse = ½. Opposite = 10 × ½ = 5.

questions.question: Convert 270° to radians.
What is the amplitude of y = −4 sin(2x + π)?
questions.explanation: Amplitude is the absolute value of the coefficient of sine: |−4| = 4.

questions.question: Which identity is the double-angle formula for cos(2θ)?
questions.explanation: The double-angle formula: cos(2θ) = cos²θ − sin²θ (also equals 2cos²θ − 1 or 1 − 2sin²θ).

questions.question: Solve sin(x) = √2/2 on [0, 2π).
questions.explanation: sin(x) = √2/2 at x = π/4 (Q1) and x = π − π/4 = 3π/4 (Q2).

questions.question: A triangle has sides a = 7, b = 10, and angle A = 40°. Using the Law of Sines, which expression gives angle B?
questions.explanation: Law of Sines: a/sin A = b/sin B → sin B = b·sin A / a = (10 sin 40°)/7.

questions.question: Given vectors u = ⟨3, −1⟩ and v = ⟨2, 4⟩, find u · v (dot product).
The period of y = 3 cos(πx) is:
questions.explanation: Period = 2π/|B| = 2π/π = 2.

questions.question: A function f has the graph shown. From the context, f is piecewise: f(x) = x + 2 for x < 1 and f(x) = 3 for x ≥ 1. State the domain, range, and evaluate f(0) + f(1).
questions.answer: Domain: all real numbers; Range: (−∞, 3] ∪ {3} = (−∞, 3]; f(0) + f(1) = 2 + 3 = 5
questions.explanation: f(0) = 0 + 2 = 2 (using first piece). f(1) = 3 (using second piece). Sum = 5. Range: x < 1 gives (−∞, 3) from x + 2; x ≥ 1 gives {3}. Combined range: (−∞, 3].

questions.question: The loudness L (in decibels) of a sound is L = 10 log(I/I₀), where I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m². A sound has intensity I = 10⁻⁴ W/m². Find L.
Verify the identity: (1 − cos²θ)/sinθ = sinθ.
questions.explanation: By the Pythagorean identity, 1 − cos²θ = sin²θ. So (sin²θ)/sinθ = sinθ (for sinθ ≠ 0).

questions.question: If f(x) = x² − 1 and g(x) = √x, what is the domain of g(f(x))?
questions.explanation: g(f(x)) = √(x² − 1). We need x² − 1 ≥ 0 → x² ≥ 1 → x ≤ −1 or x ≥ 1.

questions.question: Which of the following polynomial end behaviors describes f(x) = −2x⁴ + 3x² − 1?
options: Up on left, up on right
Down on left, up on right
Down on left, down on right
Up on left, down on right
questions.answer: Down on left, down on right
questions.explanation: The leading term is −2x⁴ (negative, even degree), so f(x) → −∞ as x → ±∞.

questions.question: An observer 50 m from the base of a building measures the angle of elevation to the top as 65°. Which expression gives the building's height?
questions.explanation: tan 65° = height/50 → height = 50 tan 65°.

questions.question: What is the reference angle for 5π/6?
questions.explanation: 5π/6 is in Q2. Reference angle = π − 5π/6 = π/6.

questions.question: The graph of y = 2sin(3x − π/2) + 1 has a phase shift of:
questions.explanation: Phase shift = (π/2)/3 = π/6. Since the form is sin(3(x − π/6)), the shift is π/6 to the right.

questions.question: Use the sum formula to find the exact value of sin(75°).
In triangle ABC, a = 8, b = 11, c = 6. Using the Law of Cosines, which expression gives cos A?
questions.explanation: Law of Cosines: a² = b² + c² − 2bc cos A → cos A = (b² + c² − a²)/(2bc) = (121 + 36 − 64)/(2 × 11 × 6).

questions.question: Vector v has magnitude 5 and direction angle 53° from the positive x-axis. What is its component form?
questions.explanation: Component form: ⟨|v|cosθ, |v|sinθ⟩ = ⟨5cos53°, 5sin53°⟩ ≈ ⟨3, 4⟩.

questions.question: The parametric equations x = 2t, y = t² describe which curve?
questions.explanation: Eliminate t: t = x/2, so y = (x/2)² = x²/4. This is a parabola.

questions.question: Convert the polar point (4, π/3) to rectangular coordinates.
Which equation represents an ellipse with semi-major axis 5 along the x-axis and semi-minor axis 3?
questions.explanation: Standard ellipse: x²/a² + y²/b² = 1, a = 5 along x-axis, b = 3. So x²/25 + y²/9 = 1.

questions.question: The 5th term of the geometric sequence 3, 6, 12, 24, … is:
questions.explanation: First term a₁ = 3, common ratio r = 2. a₅ = 3 × 2⁴ = 3 × 16 = 48.

questions.question: How many ways can a committee of 3 be chosen from 8 people?
What is the coefficient of x³ in the expansion of (x + 2)⁵?
questions.explanation: Use the Binomial Theorem: the x³ term has k=2, giving C(5,2)·x³·2² = 10·4 = 40.

questions.question: Evaluate lim(x→3) (x² − 9)/(x − 3).
Which of the following is NOT a condition for f(x) to be continuous at x = a?
questions.explanation: Continuity requires: f(a) defined, the limit exists, and limit equals f(a). There is no requirement that f(a) = 0.

questions.question: Find the focus and directrix of the parabola y = (1/8)x².
questions.explanation: Rewrite as x² = 8y. In form x² = 4py: 4p = 8 → p = 2. Focus is (0, p) = (0, 2) and directrix is y = −p = −2.

questions.question: Prove by mathematical induction that 1 + 2 + 3 + … + n = n(n+1)/2 for all positive integers n.
questions.answer: Base: n=1 → 1 = 1(2)/2 = 1 ✓. Inductive step: Assume true for n=k; show for n=k+1. Sum to k+1 = k(k+1)/2 + (k+1) = (k+1)(k/2 + 1) = (k+1)(k+2)/2 ✓.
questions.explanation: Base case verified. Inductive step: adding the (k+1)th term to the inductive hypothesis yields (k+1)(k+2)/2, which matches the formula for n = k+1.

questions.question: Describe the continuity of f(x) = (x² − 4)/(x − 2) at x = 2. Identify the type of discontinuity and redefine f(2) to make f continuous.
questions.answer: Removable discontinuity at x = 2. Redefine f(2) = 4.
questions.explanation: f(x) = (x−2)(x+2)/(x−2) = x+2 for x ≠ 2. The limit as x→2 is 4, but f(2) is undefined. This is a removable discontinuity. Setting f(2) = 4 makes f continuous everywhere.

quizQuestions.question: If f(x) = 3x − 2 and g(x) = x + 5, what is (f ∘ g)(x)?
Which of the following functions does NOT have an inverse that is also a function?
quizQuestions.explanation: f(x) = x² fails the horizontal line test over all reals, so its inverse is not a function.

quizQuestions.question: The domain of f(x) = √(x − 4) is:
quizQuestions.explanation: The radicand must be non-negative: x − 4 ≥ 0 → x ≥ 4.

quizQuestions.question: If f(x) = 2x + 6, what is f⁻¹(x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Swap x and y: x = 2y + 6 → 2y = x − 6 → y = (x − 6)/2.

quizQuestions.question: A piecewise function is defined as f(x) = x + 1 for x < 2 and f(x) = x² for x ≥ 2. What is f(2) + f(0)?
quizQuestions.explanation: f(2) = 2² = 4 (second piece). f(0) = 0 + 1 = 1 (first piece). Sum = 5.

quizQuestions.question: What does the end behavior of f(x) = 5x³ − 2x + 1 look like?
options: Down on left, up on right
Up on left, down on right
quizQuestions.answer: Down on left, up on right
quizQuestions.explanation: Odd degree with positive leading coefficient: falls to −∞ on left, rises to +∞ on right.

quizQuestions.question: How many real zeros can a polynomial of degree 4 have at most?
quizQuestions.explanation: By the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, a degree-4 polynomial has at most 4 real zeros.

quizQuestions.question: The rational function f(x) = (x − 3)/((x − 3)(x + 2)) has:
options: A vertical asymptote at x = 3 and x = −2
A hole at x = 3 and a vertical asymptote at x = −2
A hole at x = −2 and a vertical asymptote at x = 3
quizQuestions.answer: A hole at x = 3 and a vertical asymptote at x = −2
quizQuestions.explanation: The factor (x − 3) cancels, creating a hole at x = 3. The factor (x + 2) remains in the denominator, giving a vertical asymptote at x = −2.

quizQuestions.question: For f(x) = 1/(x + 4), what is the horizontal asymptote?
quizQuestions.explanation: Degree of numerator (0) is less than degree of denominator (1), so the horizontal asymptote is y = 0.

quizQuestions.question: Which polynomial has zeros at x = −1, x = 2, and x = 5?
quizQuestions.explanation: Setting each factor to zero gives x = −1, x = 2, x = 5.

quizQuestions.question: Simplify: log₃(27).
quizQuestions.explanation: log₃(27) = log₃(3³) = 3.

quizQuestions.question: Which property allows you to write log(AB) as log A + log B?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Product Rule: logₐ(MN) = logₐ M + logₐ N.

quizQuestions.question: Solve: e^x = 15. Which expression gives the exact answer?
quizQuestions.explanation: Taking the natural log of both sides: ln(eˣ) = ln 15 → x = ln 15.

quizQuestions.question: Using change-of-base, log₇(50) equals:
quizQuestions.explanation: Change-of-base formula: logₐ(b) = log(b)/log(a) = log(50)/log(7).

quizQuestions.question: A population grows logistically and approaches a carrying capacity of 10,000. Which best describes the long-term behavior?
quizQuestions.explanation: Logistic growth has an upper limit (carrying capacity). The population approaches but never exceeds 10,000.

quizQuestions.question: In a right triangle with legs 5 and 12, what is sin of the angle opposite the leg of length 5?
quizQuestions.explanation: Hypotenuse = √(25 + 144) = 13. sin = opposite/hypotenuse = 5/13.

quizQuestions.question: What is the exact value of tan(45°)?
quizQuestions.explanation: In a 45-45-90 triangle, both legs are equal, so tan(45°) = opposite/adjacent = 1.

quizQuestions.question: If cos θ = 5/13 in a right triangle, what is tan θ?
quizQuestions.explanation: cos θ = adjacent/hypotenuse = 5/13, so opposite = √(13² − 5²) = 12. tan θ = 12/5.

quizQuestions.question: The inverse trig function sin⁻¹(0.5) gives an angle of:
In a 30-60-90 triangle with hypotenuse 2, what is the side opposite the 60° angle?
quizQuestions.explanation: In a 30-60-90 triangle, sides are 1, √3, 2. With hypotenuse 2, the side opposite 60° is √3.

quizQuestions.question: Convert 5π/4 radians to degrees.
What is the reference angle for 7π/6?
quizQuestions.explanation: 7π/6 is in Q3. Reference angle = 7π/6 − π = π/6.

quizQuestions.question: On the unit circle, what are the coordinates at angle π/3?
Arc length formula is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Arc length: s = rθ, where θ must be in radians and r is the radius.

quizQuestions.question: In which quadrant is sin negative and cos positive?
quizQuestions.explanation: In Q4: x > 0 (cos positive), y < 0 (sin negative).

quizQuestions.question: What is the period of y = sin(4x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Period = 2π/|B| = 2π/4 = π/2.

quizQuestions.question: What is the amplitude of y = −5 cos(x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Amplitude = |−5| = 5. It is always a positive value.

quizQuestions.question: The graph of y = sin(x − π/4) is the graph of y = sin(x) shifted:
quizQuestions.explanation: Replacing x with (x − π/4) shifts the graph π/4 units to the right.

quizQuestions.question: Where does the graph of y = tan(x) have vertical asymptotes?
quizQuestions.explanation: tan(x) = sin(x)/cos(x) is undefined when cos(x) = 0, at x = π/2 + nπ.

quizQuestions.question: A sinusoidal model has maximum value 7 and minimum value −1. What is the amplitude and midline?
quizQuestions.explanation: Amplitude = (max − min)/2 = (7 − (−1))/2 = 4. Midline = (max + min)/2 = (7 + (−1))/2 = 3.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a Pythagorean identity?
quizQuestions.explanation: The fundamental Pythagorean identity is sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.

quizQuestions.question: Simplify: sin²x/(1 − cos²x).
quizQuestions.explanation: 1 − cos²x = sin²x by Pythagorean identity. So sin²x/sin²x = 1.

quizQuestions.question: What is sin(A + B)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Sum formula: sin(A + B) = sinA cosB + cosA sinB.

quizQuestions.question: Solve: 2cos(x) = √2 on [0, 2π).
quizQuestions.explanation: cos(x) = √2/2 at x = π/4 (Q1) and x = 7π/4 (Q4).

quizQuestions.question: The double-angle formula sin(2θ) equals:
quizQuestions.explanation: sin(2θ) = sin(θ + θ) = sinθcosθ + cosθsinθ = 2sinθcosθ.

quizQuestions.question: Which law should you use to solve a triangle when given two sides and the angle between them (SAS)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Law of Cosines is used for SAS and SSS cases in oblique triangles.

quizQuestions.question: In the ambiguous case (SSA), how many triangles exist if the given side opposite the angle is greater than the other given side?
quizQuestions.explanation: When a > b (the side opposite the given angle is larger than the other given side), exactly one triangle is formed.

quizQuestions.question: The area of a triangle with sides a = 6, b = 8, and included angle C = 30° is:
Law of Cosines: c² = a² + b² − 2ab cos C. If C = 90°, this reduces to:
quizQuestions.explanation: cos(90°) = 0, so 2ab cos(90°) = 0. The formula becomes c² = a² + b² — the Pythagorean Theorem.

quizQuestions.question: In triangle ABC, A = 30°, a = 5, b = 8. The angle B is found using Law of Sines. How many solutions exist?
quizQuestions.explanation: SSA with a < b: sin B = b sinA / a = 8(0.5)/5 = 0.8. Since 0.8 < 1, sin B has two angles on [0°, 180°), giving 2 triangles.

quizQuestions.question: What is the magnitude of vector v = ⟨−3, 4⟩?
Two vectors u and v are orthogonal if:
quizQuestions.explanation: Vectors are orthogonal (perpendicular) if and only if their dot product equals zero.

quizQuestions.question: Find the dot product of u = ⟨2, 5⟩ and v = ⟨−1, 3⟩.
Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
quizQuestions.explanation: Speed has only magnitude (no direction), making it a scalar. The others are vectors.

quizQuestions.question: A vector with magnitude 1 is called:
quizQuestions.explanation: A unit vector has magnitude 1. Any vector can be normalized by dividing by its magnitude.

quizQuestions.question: The parametric equations x = cos t, y = sin t for t ∈ [0, 2π) describe:
quizQuestions.explanation: x² + y² = cos²t + sin²t = 1. This is the equation of a unit circle.

quizQuestions.question: Convert the rectangular point (−1, √3) to polar coordinates (r, θ).
quizQuestions.explanation: r = √(1 + 3) = 2. θ = arctan(√3/−1); since the point is in Q2, θ = π − π/3 = 2π/3.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the polar equation of a circle of radius 5 centered at the origin?
quizQuestions.explanation: A circle of radius 5 centered at the origin has the simple polar equation r = 5.

quizQuestions.question: Eliminate the parameter from x = t + 1, y = 2t − 3 to get the rectangular equation.
quizQuestions.explanation: t = x − 1. Substitute: y = 2(x − 1) − 3 = 2x − 2 − 3 = 2x − 5.

quizQuestions.question: The polar equation r = 1 − cos θ represents which type of curve?
quizQuestions.explanation: r = 1 ± cos θ and r = 1 ± sin θ are cardioids — heart-shaped polar curves.

quizQuestions.question: The parabola x² = 12y opens:
quizQuestions.explanation: The form x² = 4py with p > 0 opens upward. Here 4p = 12 → p = 3 > 0.

quizQuestions.question: For the ellipse x²/16 + y²/9 = 1, the length of the major axis is:
quizQuestions.explanation: a² = 16 → a = 4. Major axis length = 2a = 8.

quizQuestions.question: What is the eccentricity of a circle?
quizQuestions.explanation: A circle has eccentricity e = 0. Ellipses: 0 < e < 1. Parabolas: e = 1. Hyperbolas: e > 1.

quizQuestions.question: Which conic section has two branches?
quizQuestions.explanation: A hyperbola consists of two separate branches opening in opposite directions.

quizQuestions.question: Completing the square in x² + 6x + y² − 4y = 12 yields a circle with center:
Find the common difference of the arithmetic sequence: 7, 11, 15, 19, …
quizQuestions.explanation: Common difference d = 11 − 7 = 4.

quizQuestions.question: What is the 10th term of the geometric sequence 2, 6, 18, …?
The sum of the first n terms of an arithmetic series with first term a₁ and last term aₙ is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Arithmetic series sum: Sₙ = n(a₁ + aₙ)/2.

quizQuestions.question: In a mathematical induction proof, what are the two required steps?
quizQuestions.explanation: Proof by induction requires: (1) Base case (usually n=1) and (2) Inductive step (assume true for n=k, prove for n=k+1).

quizQuestions.question: A geometric series has first term 5 and common ratio 1/2. The sum of the infinite series is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Infinite geometric series: S = a₁/(1 − r) = 5/(1 − 1/2) = 5/(1/2) = 10, since |r| < 1.

quizQuestions.question: How many ways can 5 books be arranged on a shelf?
quizQuestions.explanation: 5! = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120 (permutations of 5 distinct items).

quizQuestions.question: How many ways can a president and vice president be chosen from a club of 10 members?
quizQuestions.explanation: Order matters (positions are distinct): P(10, 2) = 10 × 9 = 90.

quizQuestions.question: What is C(7, 3)?
In the expansion of (a + b)⁴, the coefficient of a²b² (from Pascal's triangle) is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Row 4 of Pascal's triangle: 1, 4, 6, 4, 1. The middle term coefficient is 6 = C(4,2).

quizQuestions.question: A bag has 4 red and 6 blue marbles. The probability of drawing 2 red marbles without replacement is:
quizQuestions.explanation: P = (4/10)(3/9) = 12/90 = 2/15. Also C(4,2)/C(10,2) = 6/45 = 2/15. Both methods give the same answer.

quizQuestions.question: What does lim(x→2) f(x) = 5 mean?
options: f approaches 5 as x approaches 2 from the right only
f(x) gets arbitrarily close to 5 as x gets close to 2 from either side
quizQuestions.answer: f(x) gets arbitrarily close to 5 as x gets close to 2 from either side
quizQuestions.explanation: A limit describes the behavior of f(x) as x approaches a value, not necessarily the function value at that point.

quizQuestions.question: Evaluate: lim(x→4) (2x + 1).
quizQuestions.explanation: By direct substitution (polynomial): 2(4) + 1 = 9.

quizQuestions.question: A function f has lim(x→3⁻) f(x) = 4 and lim(x→3⁺) f(x) = 7. What is lim(x→3) f(x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal. Since 4 ≠ 7, the limit does not exist.

quizQuestions.question: What type of discontinuity does f(x) = 1/x have at x = 0?
quizQuestions.explanation: As x → 0, f(x) = 1/x → ±∞. This is an infinite (or essential) discontinuity.

quizQuestions.question: lim(x→0) (sin x)/x equals:
quizQuestions.explanation: This is a fundamental limit in calculus: lim(x→0) (sin x)/x = 1. It is proved using the squeeze theorem.
